questions.question: What best describes the architectural difference between Angular and React?
options: Angular is an opinionated, batteries-included framework using inversion of control, while React is a focused view library composed with separate routing/state libraries
Both are full frameworks, but Angular renders only on the server and React renders only on the client
Angular is a view library you compose, while React ships routing, forms, and HttpClient in-box
They are functionally identical apart from Angular using TypeScript and React using JavaScript
questions.explanation: Angular ships the stack (routing, forms, HttpClient, DI, CLI, SSR) and uses inversion of control; React is a focused view library that owns rendering and leaves the rest to separate libraries. Option 3 reverses the two roles.

questions.question: Since Angular v19, components are standalone by default, so you no longer need to write `standalone: true`, and `standalone: false` is the explicit opt-out for a declaration still owned by an NgModule.
questions.explanation: Standalone became the default in v19; the redundant `standalone: true` is removed by migration, and `standalone: false` marks legacy NgModule-declared components/directives/pipes.

questions.question: A counter's click handler runs and calls `count.update(n => n + 1)`, but the template `{{ count }}` never updates on screen. Why?
options: Binding `count` without parentheses binds the signal function object, which never registers the template as a dependency, so the view is never refreshed
`signal()` is not reactive; you must use a `BehaviorSubject` with the async pipe to update the view
`update()` does not mutate the signal; only `set()` triggers change detection
The component must use `ChangeDetectionStrategy.Default` for signal reads to refresh
questions.explanation: Reading a signal means invoking it: `{{ count() }}` returns the value and registers the template as a reactive dependency. Binding `{{ count }}` binds the function object itself, which never updates. `update()` is a valid writer, and signals drive change detection regardless of strategy.

questions.question: Why does the `@for` block require a `track` expression when `*ngFor`'s `trackBy` was optional?
options: `track` gives each item a stable identity so Angular can move and reuse existing DOM nodes instead of destroying and recreating them, and making it mandatory prevents the common untracked-list re-render bug
`track` is required only to enable the `$index` contextual variable, which `*ngFor` lacked
`track` tells the compiler the array length so it can pre-allocate DOM nodes for performance
`track` is required because `@for` cannot iterate over arrays without an explicit numeric counter
questions.explanation: `track` provides stable item identity for efficient DOM reuse; omitting it raises compiler error NG5002. The other options misstate its purpose.

questions.question: Which behaviors does the `async` pipe provide for free? (Select all that apply.)
options: Automatically subscribes and unsubscribes when the view is destroyed
Unwraps the latest emitted value, returning `null` until the first emission
Calls `markForCheck()` on every emission so OnPush and zoneless components re-render
Converts a cold HTTP Observable into a hot one shared across all subscribers
Retries a failed Observable up to three times before surfacing the error
questions.explanation: The async pipe auto-subscribes/unsubscribes, unwraps the latest value (null until first emit), and calls markForCheck() on each emission. It does not multicast cold Observables nor add automatic retry behavior.

questions.question: What does `@Input({ required: true }) prop` do, and what is `transform` for?
options: `required: true` makes Angular emit a build error if the parent omits the binding; `transform` coerces the bound value before assignment (built-ins `booleanAttribute`, `numberAttribute`)
`required: true` throws a runtime exception during change detection if the value is `null`; `transform` validates the value against a schema
`required: true` forces the input to be passed as a signal; `transform` converts an Observable input into a signal
`required: true` marks the input for two-way binding; `transform` generates the matching `propChange` output
questions.explanation: Required decorator inputs (v16+) produce a compile-time error when the binding is omitted, and `transform` coerces the incoming value (booleanAttribute/numberAttribute since v16.1). The other options describe behaviors that do not exist.

questions.question: The `output()` function returns an `EventEmitter`, the same type returned by the `@Output()` decorator.
questions.explanation: `output()` returns an `OutputEmitterRef<T>` (with `emit()` and `subscribe()` via `OutputRef`), which is neither an `EventEmitter` nor an RxJS Observable. Only the older `@Output()` decorator uses `EventEmitter`.

questions.question: An OnPush component is checked during a change-detection pass on which of the following? (Select all that apply.)
options: Its input receives a new reference for an object value
An event fires from it or one of its descendants
A signal read in its template changes
An input object is mutated in place while keeping the same reference
A sibling component (not an ancestor or descendant) re-renders
questions.explanation: OnPush re-checks on a new input reference, an own/descendant event, an async-pipe emit, a template signal change, or an explicit markForCheck(). In-place mutation keeps the same reference so it is not detected, and an unrelated sibling's render does not dirty this view.

questions.question: Why does calling `inject(SomeService)` inside `ngOnInit` throw NG0203, while the same call in a field initializer works?
options: `inject()` requires an active injection context, which exists during construction (field initializers) but not later when `ngOnInit` runs; wrap the later call in `runInInjectionContext()` to fix it
`ngOnInit` runs before the injector tree is built, so no providers are registered yet
Field initializers run on a different microtask where the injector is globally available, unlike lifecycle hooks
`inject()` only works for services provided in `root`; `ngOnInit` cannot resolve route-level providers
questions.explanation: `inject()` needs an active injection context. Field initializers run during construction while that context is active; `ngOnInit` runs after construction when no context is active, hence NG0203. The fix is to inject in a field initializer or use runInInjectionContext.

questions.question: This typeahead occasionally displays results for an older query under fast typing and variable latency. Which operator replaces `mergeMap` to fix it, and why?
options: `switchMap`, because it cancels the previous inner Observable the moment a new term arrives, so only the latest query's response reaches the subscriber
`concatMap`, because it queues requests and runs them strictly in order, guaranteeing the last typed query resolves last
`exhaustMap`, because it ignores the newest query until the in-flight request completes, ensuring freshness
`mergeMap` with a higher `concurrent` limit, because limiting concurrency prevents out-of-order resolution
questions.explanation: `switchMap` cancels the prior inner request when a new value arrives, so stale responses never overwrite newer ones. `concatMap` preserves order but still resolves stale requests; `exhaustMap` would drop the newest (most relevant) query; limiting `mergeMap` concurrency does not prevent stale ordering.

questions.question: What is the practical difference between `CanMatch` and `CanActivate` that makes `CanMatch` the right tool for feature flags and conditional lazy loading?
options: `CanMatch` runs during route matching, so returning `false` skips the route (the router tries the next) and gates whether the lazy bundle is fetched at all; `CanActivate` runs after the route is matched
`CanMatch` runs only on the client and `CanActivate` runs only during SSR, so feature flags must use `CanMatch`
`CanActivate` can return a `UrlTree` to redirect but `CanMatch` cannot, so `CanMatch` is used only to block navigation
`CanMatch` runs after the component is created and `CanActivate` runs before, so `CanMatch` sees resolved data
questions.explanation: CanMatch participates in matching: a false result skips the route and prevents the lazy loadChildren/loadComponent bundle from being fetched, ideal for feature flags. CanActivate runs after a route is matched to decide entry.

questions.question: With strictly typed reactive forms (v14+), what are the two value-type gotchas and their remedies?
options: A control's value includes `null` (because `reset()` sets `null`), dropped with `nonNullable: true`; a group's `.value` is a `Partial` because disabled controls are omitted, fixed with `getRawValue()`
A control's value is always `any` until validated, so cast it manually; a group's `.value` excludes pristine controls, fixed with `markAllAsTouched()`
A control's value type is `string` regardless of declared type, fixed via a generic on `FormControl`; disabled controls throw, so re-enable them before reading
A control's value includes `undefined` after `reset()`, fixed with `nonNullable: true`; a group's `.value` includes disabled controls by default, so use `value` directly
questions.explanation: Control values are `T | null` by default because reset() sets null (fixed with nonNullable), and a group's `.value` is a Partial because disabled controls are omitted (use getRawValue() for the complete, non-partial value).

questions.question: In what order do HTTP interceptors execute, and why must you clone the request before adding headers?
options: Requests pass through interceptors in array order toward the backend and responses flow back in reverse; `HttpRequest` is immutable (it may be re-emitted on retry), so you must `req.clone({ headers })` and pass the clone to `next()`
Interceptors run in reverse array order on the request and forward order on the response; cloning is required only to avoid sharing headers between concurrent requests
All interceptors run concurrently; cloning is required because `next()` mutates the original request object in place
Requests and responses both flow in array order; cloning is optional and only improves performance by avoiding object allocation
questions.explanation: Interceptors form nested middleware: request flows first-to-last toward the backend, response flows back in reverse. HttpRequest is immutable and may be re-emitted on retry, so headers must be added via clone() and the clone passed to next().

questions.question: A developer wraps `<app-chart>` in a `@defer` block to lazy-load a heavy charting library, but the initial bundle does not shrink. `<app-chart>` is also rendered eagerly elsewhere in the same template. Why?
options: `@defer` only splits a dependency into a lazy chunk if it is referenced solely inside `@defer` blocks in that file; because `<app-chart>` is also used eagerly, the compiler must keep it in the main chunk
`@defer` cannot lazy-load components, only modules; charting libraries require a dynamic `import()` in the component class instead
`@defer` requires the `on viewport` trigger to actually split the bundle; the default `on idle` trigger keeps dependencies eager
The chart component is not marked `standalone`, so `@defer` silently falls back to eager loading without warning
questions.explanation: A deferred dependency is only emitted as a separate chunk when it is used exclusively inside @defer blocks. Any eager usage forces it into the main bundle, so the deferred reference adds nothing. Trigger choice does not change this rule, and @defer does lazy-load standalone components.

questions.question: A developer mirrors an input into editable local state using an effect that reads `options()` and writes `selected`, resetting on every options change — but the user's edit is wiped each time options change for any reason. What is the idiomatic fix?
options: Use `linkedSignal(() => this.options()[0])`: it derives the initial value, stays writable for edits, and resets only when `options` actually changes — no effect or manual reset bookkeeping
Move the reset into `ngOnChanges` so it only fires when the input reference changes
Wrap the effect body in `untracked()` so it stops re-running when `options` changes
Replace the effect with a `computed()` so the value becomes read-only and cannot be wiped
questions.explanation: linkedSignal is purpose-built for writable-derived-with-reset state: it derives an initial value, remains writable, and resets only when the source changes. An effect-driven mirror is the anti-pattern; computed() would make the value read-only (not editable), and untracked() would break the reset semantics.

questions.question: `Cache` is provided as `RedisCache` in root and as `MemoryCache` in a lazy route's `providers`. A component under that route calls `inject(Cache)`. Which instance does it get, and how can it reach the root one?
options: It gets `MemoryCache` because resolution walks bottom-up and the first matching provider (route-level) wins; reach root with `inject(Cache, { skipSelf: true })` at the right boundary or by not providing it at the route
It gets `RedisCache` because root providers always take precedence over route providers for singletons
It throws NG0201 because two providers for the same token in different injectors is a conflict
It gets `MemoryCache`, and the only way to reach root is to re-provide `RedisCache` again inside the component
questions.explanation: Resolution walks the ElementInjector chain up through the route-level EnvironmentInjector before reaching root, and the first match wins — so the route's MemoryCache shadows root. skipSelf bypasses the nearest injector to reach the ancestor. Two providers in different injectors is legal, not NG0201.

questions.question: In a unit test for an OnPush component running under the zoneless default, what is the correct way to set an input and flush the resulting change detection?
options: Use `fixture.componentRef.setInput('user', value)` so the input pipeline marks the OnPush view dirty, then `await fixture.whenStable()` to flush rather than calling `detectChanges()` manually
Assign `fixture.componentInstance.user = value` directly, then call `fixture.detectChanges()` to flush
Mutate the existing input object in place, then call `tick()` inside `fakeAsync` to flush
Use `setInput()` and then call `fixture.detectChanges()`, which is the only flush method that works without Zone.js
questions.explanation: setInput() drives the real input pipeline so the OnPush view is marked dirty (a direct property assignment would not), and under the zoneless default you flush with await fixture.whenStable(); manually calling detectChanges() can even throw NG0100.

questions.question: How does Angular defend against XSS by default, and what is the risk of `DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrust*`?
options: Interpolation escapes text and bindings to sensitive sinks (`[innerHTML]`, `[href]`, `[style]`) are sanitized per `SecurityContext`; `bypassSecurityTrust*` disables sanitization and is safe only for values you fully control, never user input
Angular blocks all `[innerHTML]` bindings outright; `bypassSecurityTrust*` is the standard, safe way to render any HTML including user input
Sanitization happens only during SSR; on the client you must call `bypassSecurityTrust*` to re-enable it after hydration
Interpolation runs scripts but strips them at runtime; `bypassSecurityTrust*` simply caches the sanitized result for performance
questions.explanation: Angular escapes interpolated text and sanitizes bindings to sensitive sinks by SecurityContext, stripping scripts and unsafe URLs/CSS. bypassSecurityTrust* turns sanitization off, so it must only be used on fully trusted values, never user-supplied input.
